inline_comments.message: Remove asterisks from imports
I understand your intention here but in case in case of GET method this looks very strange.
Where the `handle` part is in test? This is misleading.
What `Then` section is supposed to do?
I wouldn't expect this is an assertion.
I think this can be just static method in ExternalAPIUtil

Also it's super unintuitive to put asserts here. I would never expect that when reading the test. You can, for example, create additional method assertReceived, where you can compare received and expected later.

Also, because this code is executed by tested class there can be a situation, where exceptions thorwn by those asserts will be caught.

I don't think that's the best way of implementing this method. Original buildAndThrowWorkflowException always thorws exception. This can lead to some unexpected behaviour in code that calls this method and exception isn't thrown.

Actually that would defeat the purpose of separating construction from logic and improving the classes testability. The factory is just a helper class to have:
1. A Single point of creating the ExternalAPIUtil class with its dependencies, so that clients are not burdened with having to pass all of its dependencies
2. still have DI through constructor to have the class testable by providing configurable mocks.
Done

I think this can be left as is - I know that this is not the most expressive solution syntax-wise and the best way would to do it would be to write a fluent builder for configuring the mock. On the other hand I wanted to avoid that since this is still simple enough to understand without adding too much additional code. If you have an idea for a cleaner solution, let me know.

the name of the test refers to the logic that the unit test verifies, not what is going on in the unit test. So the implementation of the tested method should handle the exceptions. I have made some small changes to hopefully improve how the exception is verified so it may be clearer now - especially the method output is now obvious
Done. Changed this to have a cleaner BDD approach with an appropriate THEN section
Done, This indeed was hard to understand - as mentioned above I changed this to have a definite and clear THEN section for assertions

Done, mostly. You are right. I wanted to mimick a mockito verification. I have changed this to mimick a thrown BpmnError with the expected values, except for the execution. This object is used inside the ExceptionUtil class and I wanted to avoid writing similar logic that would use the valuies in the same manner - that is why I've decided to leave the assertions for the execution to at least check if the same reference is passed.

As for the second remark I do not quite understand the case.

Done. I do agree, to an extent :) Basically it is true that the outcome of the tested method is a BpmnError if something fails, but this is not clear unless you actually read the method implementation - the method's signature does not indicate that a RuntimeException is thrown. And it should. So since there is no static protection, the test was not entirely broken. But when we expect that in case any error occurs a BpmnError should be unconditionally thrown - then your statement is valid. You can review my changes.

I would opt for something like:
when(httpClient.get()).thenReturn(response)
when(httpClient.post()).thenReturn(response)

My point was that test name suggest some action which should be verified. What I saw on the other hand was only function call (with hidden assert). That gave impression that mocked exception was virtually swallowed.

Now is much better.
Is this field supposed to be UUID? "UUID666" is not a correct uuid.

"UUID666" is not a correct uuid. I think it would be better to use real uuid here. Here you're testing class with incorrect data. I think behaviour is unexpected for incorrect data.

Also tests are examples of usage of class. I would show that you're expected to pass correct uuid here.

I addition name of constant is misleading - it's not an UUID.
I can't see a reason for this constant - you can just use (static imported) ACCEPTED. That would increase readability of tests as well.
I think creating tested object should be in GIVEN part, unless you're testing it.

I don't like this assert - you cannot assure that tested object will actually run this method and run this assert. Also when you use this class you cannot assute that tested object will use other method to throw exception, for example buildWorkflowException.

My suggestion would be using spy or mock here and asserting all interactions with this class.

Also this is assert in WHEN part of test.
Is this ExternalAPIUtil really util class? You create an util object that has a dependency of another util? Maybe the class should be renamed?
I do agree the name is misleading and the 'util' suffix does not indicate the classes responsibilities. At this point I would prefer no to do any major refactors and renames.
Done
Done
in my opinion this approach is more readable by keeping both the instance creation and invocation of tested method together. This way test data and mocks are not mixed together with the instance.

I do agree that this solution may seem controversial, but in the end this is a simplified version of mock verification. Using mockito would solve this on a more readable level. Without it I wanted to make sure that the passed execution is at least the same reference without reinventing the wheel and writing an overblown mock.
Tests shouldn't be randomized. Change it to one fixed UUID.
Done

In other places in our code, we call the HttpClient constructor directly.  Here we have a new factory.  At present, the factory doesn't add any value... it just calls the constructor. Do you foresee a case where the factory would do something additional?  If so, shouldn't we use the factory everywhere?  If not, then I think we should remove this factory.

Hi Rob,
One of the main reasons I introduced the HttpClientFactory, is to provide a way to actually break up the tight coupling between the classes that require the HttpClient and make them testable (in this case ExternalApiUtil). As you have mentioned all places that require HttpClient call the constructor directly. This breaks SRP of those classes since they are also responsible for constructing the HttpClient.
By introducing DI the class can be tested on a unit level in isolation without using the implementation that should not be part of its function.
Of course this could be done by injecting the HttpClient itself via the constructor. Unfortunately that would require constructing the HttpClient object eagerly, more than once and with different arguments. This is where the HttpClientFactory provides another benefit -> it can construct the HttpClient lazily/on demand since you only inject the lightweight factory.

It is true that the factory should be used in all places. By introducing it, the rules for constructing the object have changed and need to be applied everywhere for a consistent approach. I have planned to do this as part of a separate commit, to avoid too many changes at once.
I would also like to extend the factory with additional methods for constructing the HttpClient for each Media type (json/xml) This would reduce the number of passed arguments to just two and remove usage duplication.

Thanks :)

Hi Rob,
please respond to the Michal comment, do you agree for these changes? As Michal wrote, if we want to test ExternalApiUtil class, we need to make HttpClient as class field to mock it. In other hand we can not create HttpClient in constructor because HttpClient is used more than once, with different arguments. This is the point for introducing factory, it creates HttpClient on demand, and we can mock this factory to mock HttpClient. Please re-think it.
